1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses in the plains of Moab by Jordan near Jericho, saying," The witnesses note God now providing for the Levites, who had no tribal inheritance, after the land's borders and division were set. The witnesses note that Gill notes that, having described the land's borders and the division among the tribes, God makes provision for the Levites; though they had no inheritance as a tribe, it was proper they should have cities and houses to dwell in. The witnesses note that the word came in the plains of Moab, on the edge of the land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — in the plains of Moab; the for the Levites — the tribe without a land-portion; the cities to dwell in — provided among all the tribes; the God's servants maintained — the people providing for the ministers.

The witnesses agree the provision for Levi shows God's care for those given wholly to his service: the tribe that received no portion of the land, having the LORD himself for their inheritance, was yet not to be left without dwelling, but provided cities among all the tribes; the order of it — the land first divided, then the Levites' cities appointed out of every tribe's portion — teaches that the servants of God are to be maintained by the people they serve, a charge laid on the whole nation to provide for the ministers of holy things.

So the witnesses make the verse provision for Levi: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: in the plains of Moab. The witnesses note the for the Levites: the tribe without a land-portion. The witnesses note the cities to dwell in: provided among all the tribes. The witnesses note the God's servants maintained: the people providing for the ministers. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: in the plains of Moab. The expositors observe the for the Levites: the tribe without a land-portion. The expositors observe the cities to dwell in: provided among all the tribes. The expositors observe the God's servants maintained: the people providing for the ministers. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD spake, the for the Levites, the cities to dwell in, the God's servants maintained, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that God provided cities for the Levites, who had no tribal inheritance — his care for those given to his service, the people charged to maintain the ministers of holy things.
2:
"Command the children of Israel, that they give unto the Levites of the inheritance of their possession cities to dwell in; and ye shall give also unto the Levites suburbs for the cities round about them." The witnesses note the command — not a request but a strict charge — that all the tribes give the Levites cities with suburbs. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is not a bare instruction, much less a request left to their option, but strictly enjoined by the Lord, who had freely given them all they should possess and had a right to all they had. The witnesses note that they were to give cities with their surrounding suburbs. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the command the children — a charge, not a request; the give... cities — provided out of every tribe's portion; the with suburbs — the land round about for their use; the rendering back to God — giving a part of what he first gave.

The witnesses agree the giving of the Levite cities was a duty owed, not a favour offered: God, who had freely given Israel the whole land, had every right to require a portion of it back for his servants, and so commanded — not asked — that the tribes provide for the Levites; the lesson is that what we give to God and his ministers is but rendering back a part of what he first gave us, a debt of gratitude and duty, not a thing left to our pleasure.

So the witnesses make the verse the strict charge: The witnesses note the command the children: a charge, not a request. The witnesses note the give... cities: provided out of every tribe's portion. The witnesses note the with suburbs: the land round about for their use. The witnesses note the rendering back to God: giving a part of what he first gave. The expositors observe the command the children: a charge, not a request. The expositors observe the give... cities: provided out of every tribe's portion. The expositors observe the with suburbs: the land round about for their use. The expositors observe the rendering back to God: giving a part of what he first gave. The believer is taught to mark the command the children, the give... cities, the with suburbs, the rendering back to God, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that God commanded — not asked — that the tribes give the Levites cities and suburbs — what we give his servants but rendering back a part of what he first gave us.
3:
"And the cities shall they have to dwell in; and the suburbs of them shall be for their cattle, and for their goods, and for all their beasts." The witnesses note the cities for the Levites to dwell in, and the suburbs for their cattle and goods. The witnesses note that Gill notes the cities were for them and their families, having no trades nor worldly business to carry on. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes "cattle" denotes oxen and beasts of burden, and "goods" the sheep and goats. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the cities to dwell in — for the Levites and their families; the suburbs for cattle — pasture for their flocks and herds; the no worldly trade — free for the sanctuary's work; the a competency — neither riches nor want, but enough.

The witnesses agree the provision was complete and fitting for a people set apart to God's service: the Levites had cities to dwell in and suburbs for their flocks and herds, enough for their living without secular trade, that they might give themselves to the work of the sanctuary; the arrangement shows God's wisdom in providing for his ministers a competency — neither riches nor want — that they be free of worldly business and at leisure for the things of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the cities and suburbs: The witnesses note the cities to dwell in: for the Levites and their families. The witnesses note the suburbs for cattle: pasture for their flocks and herds. The witnesses note the no worldly trade: free for the sanctuary's work. The witnesses note the a competency: neither riches nor want, but enough. The expositors observe the cities to dwell in: for the Levites and their families. The expositors observe the suburbs for cattle: pasture for their flocks and herds. The expositors observe the no worldly trade: free for the sanctuary's work. The expositors observe the a competency: neither riches nor want, but enough. The believer is taught to mark the cities to dwell in, the suburbs for cattle, the no worldly trade, the a competency, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that the Levites had cities to dwell in and suburbs for their cattle — a fitting competency, that the ministers of God be free of worldly business and at leisure for his service.
4:
"And the suburbs of the cities, which ye shall give unto the Levites, shall reach from the wall of the city and outward a thousand cubits round about." The witnesses note the suburbs measured a thousand cubits from the city wall outward, fixed by God's appointment. The witnesses note that Gill notes the dimensions were not left to the Israelites to give what they pleased, but fixed to a set length. The witnesses note that Clarke reads the suburbs as reaching a thousand cubits round about from the wall. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a thousand cubits — from the wall outward; the round about — the suburb on every side; the fixed by God — not left to the tribes' pleasure; the a sure rule — the Levites' portion secured from partiality.

The witnesses agree the fixing of the measure guarded the Levites' portion from the grudging or partiality of the tribes: God himself set the bounds, a thousand cubits round, that none might give less than was due nor the Levites be defrauded; the precise appointment shows that God provides for his servants by a sure rule, not leaving their maintenance to the changing goodwill of men, but securing it by his own command — a portion measured and certain.

So the witnesses make the verse the measured suburbs: The witnesses note the a thousand cubits: from the wall outward. The witnesses note the round about: the suburb on every side. The witnesses note the fixed by God: not left to the tribes' pleasure. The witnesses note the a sure rule: the Levites' portion secured from partiality. The expositors observe the a thousand cubits: from the wall outward. The expositors observe the round about: the suburb on every side. The expositors observe the fixed by God: not left to the tribes' pleasure. The expositors observe the a sure rule: the Levites' portion secured from partiality. The believer is taught to mark the a thousand cubits, the round about, the fixed by God, the a sure rule, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that the suburbs reached a thousand cubits from the wall, fixed by God — the Levites' portion secured by a sure rule, not left to the grudging goodwill of men.
5:
"And ye shall measure from without the city on the east side two thousand cubits... the city shall be in the midst: this shall be to them the suburbs of the cities." The witnesses note a further two thousand cubits measured on each side, the city set in the midst. The witnesses note that Gill notes that beyond the first thousand, two thousand more were added on each side — the first thousand for cattle and goods, the two thousand for gardens, orchards, fields, and vineyards. The witnesses note that the city stood in the midst of its measured ground. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the two thousand cubits — added on each side; the for fields and vineyards — beyond the cattle-ground; the the city in the midst — set in the centre of the square; the amply provided — god not stinting his servants.

The witnesses agree the larger measure provided amply for the Levites' living: beyond the first thousand cubits for their cattle, two thousand more on every side gave them fields and vineyards, the city set in the centre of a generous square; the fullness of the provision shows that God did not stint his servants but gave them room and means enough, the ministers of the sanctuary well provided that they might serve without distraction or want.

So the witnesses make the verse the fuller measure: The witnesses note the two thousand cubits: added on each side. The witnesses note the for fields and vineyards: beyond the cattle-ground. The witnesses note the the city in the midst: set in the centre of the square. The witnesses note the amply provided: God not stinting his servants. The expositors observe the two thousand cubits: added on each side. The expositors observe the for fields and vineyards: beyond the cattle-ground. The expositors observe the the city in the midst: set in the centre of the square. The expositors observe the amply provided: God not stinting his servants. The believer is taught to mark the two thousand cubits, the for fields and vineyards, the the city in the midst, the amply provided, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that two thousand cubits more were measured on each side, the city in the midst — God amply providing for the Levites, the ministers well supplied that they serve without want.
6:
"And among the cities which ye shall give unto the Levites there shall be six cities for refuge, which ye shall appoint for the manslayer, that he may flee thither..." The witnesses note six of the Levite cities to be cities of refuge for the manslayer. The witnesses note that Gill notes the number of the cities is afterward expressed. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the cities of refuge were assigned among the Levites, partly because they would be the most proper and impartial judges between manslayers and wilful murderers, and because their cities were dispersed through the land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the six cities — among the Levite cities; the for the manslayer — that he may flee thither; the among the Levites — impartial judges, dispersed through the land; the pointing to Christ — priest and refuge meeting in him.

The witnesses agree the placing of the refuge-cities among the Levites was full of fitness: the priests and Levites, learned in the law and dispersed through the land, would be impartial judges of the manslayer's case and ready helpers of the fugitive; that the cities of mercy were the cities of God's ministers points beyond itself to Christ, in whom the office of the Levite and the city of refuge meet — the believer fleeing to the great High Priest for sanctuary from the avenging law.

So the witnesses make the verse cities of refuge: The witnesses note the six cities: among the Levite cities. The witnesses note the for the manslayer: that he may flee thither. The witnesses note the among the Levites: impartial judges, dispersed through the land. The witnesses note the pointing to Christ: priest and refuge meeting in him. The expositors observe the six cities: among the Levite cities. The expositors observe the for the manslayer: that he may flee thither. The expositors observe the among the Levites: impartial judges, dispersed through the land. The expositors observe the pointing to Christ: priest and refuge meeting in him. The believer is taught to mark the six cities, the for the manslayer, the among the Levites, the pointing to Christ, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that six of the Levite cities were cities of refuge — fitly among God's ministers, impartial judges and helpers of the fugitive, pointing to Christ in whom priest and refuge meet.
7:
"So all the cities which ye shall give to the Levites shall be forty and eight cities: them shall ye give with their suburbs." The witnesses note the full number of the Levite cities — forty-eight, with their suburbs. The witnesses note that Gill notes the names of these forty-eight cities, the tribes they were in, and which were cities of refuge, are given elsewhere (Josh. 21). The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the forty-eight cities with their suburbs. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the forty-eight cities — the full number of Levi; the with their suburbs — each with its measured ground; the through all the tribes — the Levites dispersed everywhere; the a scattering blessed — the law and worship reaching all the land.

The witnesses agree the forty-eight cities, scattered through all the tribes, set the Levites in the midst of the whole people: not gathered in one place but dispersed, they were a leaven of instruction and worship throughout Israel, the ministers of God near at hand in every quarter; the dispersion of Levi, foretold in Jacob's word as a scattering, was turned to a blessing — the tribe spread abroad that the knowledge of the law and the service of God might reach all the land.

So the witnesses make the verse the forty-eight cities: The witnesses note the forty-eight cities: the full number of Levi. The witnesses note the with their suburbs: each with its measured ground. The witnesses note the through all the tribes: the Levites dispersed everywhere. The witnesses note the a scattering blessed: the law and worship reaching all the land. The expositors observe the forty-eight cities: the full number of Levi. The expositors observe the with their suburbs: each with its measured ground. The expositors observe the through all the tribes: the Levites dispersed everywhere. The expositors observe the a scattering blessed: the law and worship reaching all the land. The believer is taught to mark the forty-eight cities, the with their suburbs, the through all the tribes, the a scattering blessed, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that the Levite cities were forty-eight, scattered through all the tribes — the dispersion of Levi turned to a blessing, the ministers of God near at hand in every quarter (Josh. 21).
8:
"And the cities which ye shall give shall be of the possession of the children of Israel: from them that have many ye shall give many; but from them that have few ye shall give few..." The witnesses note the cities given in proportion — more from the larger tribes, fewer from the smaller. The witnesses note that Gill notes the cities came of what fell by lot to each tribe, and they were not to refuse nor grudge to give them, the land being God's, they holding it under him. The witnesses note that each tribe gave according to its portion. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the of the possession — out of each tribe's own portion; the many from the many — the larger tribes giving more; the few from the few — the smaller giving less; the the land God's — the tribes his tenants, not to grudge.

The witnesses agree the rule of proportion made the burden fair and the duty plain: each tribe gave to the Levites out of its own inheritance, the larger giving more and the smaller less, none bearing an undue share; and the ground of the giving was that the land was God's, the tribes but his tenants — so they were neither to refuse nor grudge, but cheerfully render to his servants out of what he had lent them, the whole nation jointly maintaining the ministers of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the proportioned giving: The witnesses note the of the possession: out of each tribe's own portion. The witnesses note the many from the many: the larger tribes giving more. The witnesses note the few from the few: the smaller giving less. The witnesses note the the land God's: the tribes his tenants, not to grudge. The expositors observe the of the possession: out of each tribe's own portion. The expositors observe the many from the many: the larger tribes giving more. The expositors observe the few from the few: the smaller giving less. The expositors observe the the land God's: the tribes his tenants, not to grudge. The believer is taught to mark the of the possession, the many from the many, the few from the few, the the land God's, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that the cities were given in proportion, more from the larger tribes — the land being God's and the tribes his tenants, all to render cheerfully to his servants out of what he had lent them.
9:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note God continuing his word, now to order the cities of refuge — full of both good law and pure gospel. The witnesses note that Gill notes God continued his speech to Moses. The witnesses note that Henry finds in the orders concerning the cities of refuge, fitly annexed because they were Levites' cities, a great deal both of good law in the case of murder and manslaughter, and of pure gospel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — continuing his word; the the cities of refuge — fitly annexed to the Levites' cities; the good law — the just handling of bloodshed; the pure gospel — the refuge a type of Christ.

The puritan expositor (Henry) opens the double treasure of what follows: in the law of the cities of refuge there is "good law" — the wise and just handling of bloodshed, distinguishing the murderer from the manslayer — and "pure gospel," the city of refuge being a lively type of Christ, to whom the sinner flees from the avenging justice of God; the careful provision both upholds the sanctity of human life and shadows the great salvation, justice and mercy meeting in the appointed refuge.

So the witnesses make the verse good law and gospel: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: continuing his word. The witnesses note the the cities of refuge: fitly annexed to the Levites' cities. The witnesses note the good law: the just handling of bloodshed. The witnesses note the pure gospel: the refuge a type of Christ. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: continuing his word. The expositors observe the the cities of refuge: fitly annexed to the Levites' cities. The expositors observe the good law: the just handling of bloodshed. The expositors observe the pure gospel: the refuge a type of Christ. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD spake, the the cities of refuge, the good law, the pure gospel, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that God spake to order the cities of refuge — full of good law and pure gospel, justice and mercy meeting in the appointed refuge, a type of Christ.
10:
"Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, When ye be come over Jordan into the land of Canaan;" The witnesses note the law of refuge given for the land they were about to enter. The witnesses note that Gill notes the word was now spoken directly to the children of Israel. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the time — when they were come over Jordan into Canaan, then they should appoint the cities of refuge. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the speak unto Israel — the law given to all; the when ye be come over Jordan — for their settled life in Canaan; the appointed in the land — the standing provision ordained; the God's forethought — mercy and justice ordained before they entered.

The witnesses agree the law looked forward to the settled life in the land: the cities of refuge were to be appointed once Israel had crossed Jordan and possessed Canaan, a standing provision for the nation's ordered life; the timing shows God's forethought for his people's future, ordaining mercy and justice for the land before they entered it, that from the first their common life might be governed by his righteous and gracious law.

So the witnesses make the verse for the land to come: The witnesses note the speak unto Israel: the law given to all. The witnesses note the when ye be come over Jordan: for their settled life in Canaan. The witnesses note the appointed in the land: the standing provision ordained. The witnesses note the God's forethought: mercy and justice ordained before they entered. The expositors observe the speak unto Israel: the law given to all. The expositors observe the when ye be come over Jordan: for their settled life in Canaan. The expositors observe the appointed in the land: the standing provision ordained. The expositors observe the God's forethought: mercy and justice ordained before they entered. The believer is taught to mark the speak unto Israel, the when ye be come over Jordan, the appointed in the land, the God's forethought, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that the law of refuge was given for when they crossed into Canaan — God's forethought for his people's ordered life, ordaining mercy and justice before they entered the land.
11:
"Then ye shall appoint you cities to be cities of refuge for you; that the slayer may flee thither, which killeth any person at unawares." The witnesses note the cities appointed for the one who kills a person unawares, that he may flee thither. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Jewish writers say these were to be middling cities, set where there were markets, water, and people, with good roads kept to them. The witnesses note that they were for the slayer who killed any person at unawares — by accident, without intent. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the appoint cities of refuge — the places of safety; the that the slayer may flee — the fugitive's sanctuary; the at unawares — by accident, without malice; the the way made plain — mercy easy of approach (Heb. 6:18).

The witnesses agree the refuge was for the unintentional slayer, not the murderer: he who killed "at unawares," by misadventure and without malice, might flee to the city and be safe; the care taken — that the cities be accessible, well-watered, and the roads kept clear — shows God's mercy made easy of approach, the way to refuge plain and open, as the way of salvation in Christ is made plain that the fleeing sinner may readily find it (cf. Heb. 6:18, fled for refuge).

So the witnesses make the verse refuge for the unaware: The witnesses note the appoint cities of refuge: the places of safety. The witnesses note the that the slayer may flee: the fugitive's sanctuary. The witnesses note the at unawares: by accident, without malice. The witnesses note the the way made plain: mercy easy of approach (Heb. 6:18). The expositors observe the appoint cities of refuge: the places of safety. The expositors observe the that the slayer may flee: the fugitive's sanctuary. The expositors observe the at unawares: by accident, without malice. The expositors observe the the way made plain: mercy easy of approach (Heb. 6:18). The believer is taught to mark the appoint cities of refuge, the that the slayer may flee, the at unawares, the the way made plain, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that the cities were appointed for the slayer who killed unawares — God's mercy made easy of approach, the way to refuge plain, as the way of salvation in Christ (Heb. 6:18).
12:
"And they shall be unto you cities for refuge from the avenger; that the manslayer die not, until he stand before the congregation in judgment." The witnesses note the cities a refuge from the avenger of blood, until the slayer be judged before the congregation. The witnesses note that Gill notes the avenger was the near kinsman, to whom belonged the revenging of the blood of one killed, as the right of redemption belonged to such a one. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the city was not a perpetual asylum but a refuge pro tempore, till the case could be heard. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the refuge from the avenger — the near kinsman of the slain; the that he die not — spared from rash vengeance; the until... judgment — till his cause be examined; the mercy and justice — safety, not impunity.

The witnesses agree the refuge gave not licence but a fair trial: the slayer was sheltered from the avenger of blood — the near kinsman who in hot pursuit might else have killed him — only until he could "stand before the congregation in judgment," that his cause be examined; the provision balances mercy and justice, sparing the fugitive from rash vengeance yet bringing him to a just hearing, so that neither the innocent perish unheard nor the guilty escape — the refuge a place of safety, not of impunity.

So the witnesses make the verse refuge until judgment: The witnesses note the refuge from the avenger: the near kinsman of the slain. The witnesses note the that he die not: spared from rash vengeance. The witnesses note the until... judgment: till his cause be examined. The witnesses note the mercy and justice: safety, not impunity. The expositors observe the refuge from the avenger: the near kinsman of the slain. The expositors observe the that he die not: spared from rash vengeance. The expositors observe the until... judgment: till his cause be examined. The expositors observe the mercy and justice: safety, not impunity. The believer is taught to mark the refuge from the avenger, the that he die not, the until... judgment, the mercy and justice, and to flee to the refuge God has provided. The verse thus records that the cities sheltered the slayer from the avenger until he stood in judgment — mercy and justice balanced, the refuge a place of safety and fair trial, not of impunity.
13:
"And of these cities which ye shall give six cities shall ye have for refuge." The witnesses note six of the forty-eight Levite cities set apart for refuge. The witnesses note that Gill notes these six were of the forty-eight cities given to the Levites. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set three on each side of Jordan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the six cities — of the forty-eight; the for refuge — set apart for the manslayer; the three each side — dispersed across Jordan; the measured to the need — neither stinted nor excessive; the refuge near at hand — within reach of every part of the land.

The witnesses agree the six refuge-cities, drawn from the Levites' forty-eight, were enough and well placed for the need: dispersed three on each side of Jordan, they stood within reach of every part of the land, that no fugitive be too far from safety; the choosing of six out of the whole shows that mercy had its appointed and sufficient places, the provision neither stinted nor excessive, but measured to the people's need, refuge always near at hand.

So the witnesses make the verse six cities for refuge: The witnesses note the six cities: of the forty-eight. The witnesses note the for refuge: set apart for the manslayer. The witnesses note the three each side: dispersed across Jordan. The witnesses note the measured to the need: neither stinted nor excessive. The witnesses note the refuge near at hand: within reach of every part of the land. The expositors observe the six cities: of the forty-eight. The expositors observe the for refuge: set apart for the manslayer. The expositors observe the three each side: dispersed across Jordan. The expositors observe the measured to the need: neither stinted nor excessive. The expositors observe the refuge near at hand: within reach of every part of the land. The believer is taught to mark the six cities, the for refuge, the three each side, the measured to the need, the refuge near at hand, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that six of the Levite cities were set apart for refuge — mercy with its appointed and sufficient places, refuge always near at hand for the fugitive.
14:
"Ye shall give three cities on this side Jordan, and three cities shall ye give in the land of Canaan, which shall be cities of refuge." The witnesses note three refuge-cities east of Jordan and three in Canaan. The witnesses note that Gill names the three on the east — Bezer in Reuben, Ramoth in Gilead in Gad, and Golan in Bashan in Manasseh. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the Hebrew "on this side" is fixed by the contrast with "in the land of Canaan." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the three east of Jordan — bezer, Ramoth, Golan; the three in Canaan — for the western tribes; the evenly divided — refuge for all the tribes alike; the impartial mercy — near to every one wherever they dwell.

The witnesses agree the even division of the cities, three on each side of Jordan, made refuge equally available to all the tribes: the two and a half tribes east of Jordan had their three, and the nine and a half in Canaan theirs, so that none was favoured and none neglected; the impartial spreading of mercy through the whole land teaches that God's provision of refuge is for all his people alike, near to every one, wherever they dwell.

So the witnesses make the verse three on each side: The witnesses note the three east of Jordan: Bezer, Ramoth, Golan. The witnesses note the three in Canaan: for the western tribes. The witnesses note the evenly divided: refuge for all the tribes alike. The witnesses note the impartial mercy: near to every one wherever they dwell. The expositors observe the three east of Jordan: Bezer, Ramoth, Golan. The expositors observe the three in Canaan: for the western tribes. The expositors observe the evenly divided: refuge for all the tribes alike. The expositors observe the impartial mercy: near to every one wherever they dwell. The believer is taught to mark the three east of Jordan, the three in Canaan, the evenly divided, the impartial mercy, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that three refuge-cities stood east of Jordan and three in Canaan — mercy spread impartially through the whole land, near to all God's people wherever they dwell.
15:
"These six cities shall be a refuge, both for the children of Israel, and for the stranger, and for the sojourner among them: that every one that killeth any person unawares may flee thither." The witnesses note the refuge open to the stranger and sojourner as well as the native Israelite. The witnesses note that Gill notes the refuge was for the Israelite and the proselyte of righteousness alike, there being one law in things civil and religious. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes "stranger" (ger) properly denotes a foreigner dwelling among them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the for the children of Israel — the native born; the and for the stranger — the foreigner dwelling among them; the one law for all — sheltering native and sojourner alike; the mercy without bound — the door open to Jew and Gentile (Eph. 2:13).

The witnesses agree the openness of the refuge to the stranger preaches the breadth of God's mercy: not the native Israelite only but the foreigner and sojourner who killed unawares might flee to the city and be safe, one law sheltering all alike; the welcome of the stranger to the place of refuge foreshadows the gospel grace that knows no national bound, the door of mercy in Christ open to Jew and Gentile, "to every one that killeth any person unawares," whoever he be (cf. Eph. 2:13).

So the witnesses make the verse refuge for the stranger: The witnesses note the for the children of Israel: the native born. The witnesses note the and for the stranger: the foreigner dwelling among them. The witnesses note the one law for all: sheltering native and sojourner alike. The witnesses note the mercy without bound: the door open to Jew and Gentile (Eph. 2:13). The expositors observe the for the children of Israel: the native born. The expositors observe the and for the stranger: the foreigner dwelling among them. The expositors observe the one law for all: sheltering native and sojourner alike. The expositors observe the mercy without bound: the door open to Jew and Gentile (Eph. 2:13). The believer is taught to mark the for the children of Israel, the and for the stranger, the one law for all, the mercy without bound, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that the refuge was open to the stranger and sojourner as to the Israelite — the breadth of God's mercy, one law for all, foreshadowing the grace in Christ open to Jew and Gentile (Eph. 2:13).
16:
"And if he smite him with an instrument of iron, so that he die, he is a murderer: the murderer shall surely be put to death." The witnesses note the first mark of murder — smiting with a deadly iron instrument, which proves intent to kill. The witnesses note that Gill notes this means an instrument such as a hatchet, hammer, sword, or knife. The witnesses note that Calvin observes God seems to contradict himself, absolving the involuntary slayer further on, yet here condemning whoever smites with iron, stone, or wood; the resolution is that the weapon used betrays the intent. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the an instrument of iron — a deadly weapon used; the so that he die — the fatal blow; the he is a murderer — the intent betrayed by the means; the justice on the malice — the weapon a witness to the will.

The reformed reader (Calvin) resolves the seeming difficulty: the deadly weapon — iron, a great stone, a heavy club — is taken as evidence of murderous intent, for a man does not strike with such but to kill; so the law does not punish the accident but the malice that the instrument reveals, distinguishing the murderer from the manslayer by the means and the manner of the blow, that justice fall on the guilty heart and not on mischance — the weapon a witness to the will.

So the witnesses make the verse the mark of murder: The witnesses note the an instrument of iron: a deadly weapon used. The witnesses note the so that he die: the fatal blow. The witnesses note the he is a murderer: the intent betrayed by the means. The witnesses note the justice on the malice: the weapon a witness to the will. The expositors observe the an instrument of iron: a deadly weapon used. The expositors observe the so that he die: the fatal blow. The expositors observe the he is a murderer: the intent betrayed by the means. The expositors observe the justice on the malice: the weapon a witness to the will. The believer is taught to mark the an instrument of iron, the so that he die, the he is a murderer, the justice on the malice, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that smiting with a deadly iron instrument marks the murderer — the weapon betraying the intent, justice falling on the murderous will and not on mischance.
17:
"And if he smite him with throwing a stone, wherewith he may die, and he die, he is a murderer..." The witnesses note the second mark — smiting with a stone large enough to kill, which likewise proves murder. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Jews interpret this of a stone big enough to fill a man's hand, sufficient to kill. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the murderer surely put to death. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the throwing a stone — a stone large enough to kill; the wherewith he may die — its deadly fitness; the he is a murderer — the intent shown by the weapon; the the deadly means — convicting the murderous heart.

The witnesses agree the size and deadliness of the stone, like the iron, betray the intent to kill: a man who strikes with a stone "wherewith he may die" — one large enough to be lethal — shows by his choice of weapon that he meant death, and so is a murderer; the law weighs not the material only but its deadly fitness, that the true intent be judged, the deadly means convicting the heart that wielded it of murderous purpose.

So the witnesses make the verse the deadly stone: The witnesses note the throwing a stone: a stone large enough to kill. The witnesses note the wherewith he may die: its deadly fitness. The witnesses note the he is a murderer: the intent shown by the weapon. The witnesses note the the deadly means: convicting the murderous heart. The expositors observe the throwing a stone: a stone large enough to kill. The expositors observe the wherewith he may die: its deadly fitness. The expositors observe the he is a murderer: the intent shown by the weapon. The expositors observe the the deadly means: convicting the murderous heart. The believer is taught to mark the throwing a stone, the wherewith he may die, the he is a murderer, the the deadly means, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that smiting with a deadly stone marks the murderer — the deadly means betraying the intent, the law judging the true purpose of the heart that wielded it.
18:
"Or if he smite him with an hand weapon of wood, wherewith he may die, and he die, he is a murderer: the murderer shall surely be put to death." The witnesses note the third mark — smiting with a deadly wooden weapon, again the sign of murder. The witnesses note that Gill notes a hand weapon of wood, such as a stick, staff, or club. The witnesses note that here too, if the weapon could kill and the man died, the smiter is a murderer. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a hand weapon of wood — a club fit to kill; the wherewith he may die — its lethal force; the he is a murderer — the deadly instrument the proof; the the rule fixed — the manner revealing the heart.

The witnesses agree the same principle runs through all three instances — iron, stone, and wood: in each, a weapon fit to kill, used to strike, convicts the smiter of murder, for the deadly instrument shows the deadly intent; the threefold repetition fixes the rule beyond doubt, that the manner of the killing reveals the heart, and that whoever takes a lethal weapon against his neighbour and slays him shall surely die, the sanctity of life guarded by the certain punishment of murder.

So the witnesses make the verse the deadly club: The witnesses note the a hand weapon of wood: a club fit to kill. The witnesses note the wherewith he may die: its lethal force. The witnesses note the he is a murderer: the deadly instrument the proof. The witnesses note the the rule fixed: the manner revealing the heart. The expositors observe the a hand weapon of wood: a club fit to kill. The expositors observe the wherewith he may die: its lethal force. The expositors observe the he is a murderer: the deadly instrument the proof. The expositors observe the the rule fixed: the manner revealing the heart. The believer is taught to mark the a hand weapon of wood, the wherewith he may die, the he is a murderer, the the rule fixed, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that smiting with a deadly wooden weapon marks the murderer — the threefold rule fixed, the lethal instrument revealing the heart, the sanctity of life guarded by sure punishment.
19:
"The revenger of blood himself shall slay the murderer: when he meeteth him, he shall slay him." The witnesses note the avenger of blood as the appointed executioner of the convicted murderer. The witnesses note that Gill notes the avenger not only had power but seemed obliged to slay the murderer, yet not before the case was heard and judged; the Targum adds "in judgment," when condemned by the court. The witnesses note that he was to execute the sentence when he met him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the revenger of blood — the near kinsman of the slain; the shall slay the murderer — the appointed executioner; the not before judgment — only the condemned, in due order; the public duty — justice done, not private passion.

The witnesses agree the avenger's right was no licence for private revenge but the execution of a lawful sentence: only after the murderer was condemned by the court might the avenger slay him, the kinsman acting as the appointed executioner of justice, not as a private avenger of his own passion; the arrangement satisfied both the natural claim of the slain man's blood and the order of law, the punishment of murder being a public duty carried out by the nearest hand, yet under the rule of judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the avenger as executioner: The witnesses note the the revenger of blood: the near kinsman of the slain. The witnesses note the shall slay the murderer: the appointed executioner. The witnesses note the not before judgment: only the condemned, in due order. The witnesses note the public duty: justice done, not private passion. The expositors observe the the revenger of blood: the near kinsman of the slain. The expositors observe the shall slay the murderer: the appointed executioner. The expositors observe the not before judgment: only the condemned, in due order. The expositors observe the public duty: justice done, not private passion. The believer is taught to mark the the revenger of blood, the shall slay the murderer, the not before judgment, the public duty, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that the avenger of blood was to slay the convicted murderer — the kinsman the appointed executioner of a lawful sentence, justice done under the rule of judgment, not private revenge.
20:
"But if he thrust him of hatred, or hurl at him by laying of wait, that he die;" The witnesses note hatred and lying in wait as proofs of murder, even apart from a named weapon. The witnesses note that Gill notes this still speaks of one who shall die for murder — thrusting of hatred, or pushing him down from a high place. The witnesses note that the malice and the ambush mark the deed as murder. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thrust of hatred — the settled enmity; the laying of wait — the premeditated ambush; the that he die — the murderous deed; the the heart judged — hatred the root of murder (1 John 3:15).

The witnesses agree the law looks past the instrument to the motive and the manner: where there is hatred or lying in wait, the killing is murder however done, for the settled enmity and the premeditation prove the murderous heart; the law thus reaches the inward malice and the deliberate plot, not only the deadly weapon, teaching that God judges the heart's hatred as the root of murder (cf. 1 John 3:15, whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer), the ambush and the enmity condemning the deed.

So the witnesses make the verse hatred and ambush: The witnesses note the thrust of hatred: the settled enmity. The witnesses note the laying of wait: the premeditated ambush. The witnesses note the that he die: the murderous deed. The witnesses note the the heart judged: hatred the root of murder (1 John 3:15). The expositors observe the thrust of hatred: the settled enmity. The expositors observe the laying of wait: the premeditated ambush. The expositors observe the that he die: the murderous deed. The expositors observe the the heart judged: hatred the root of murder (1 John 3:15). The believer is taught to mark the thrust of hatred, the laying of wait, the that he die, the the heart judged, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that hatred and lying in wait mark murder however done — the law reaching the inward malice, God judging the heart's hatred as the root of murder (1 John 3:15).
21:
"Or in enmity smite him with his hand, that he die... the revenger of blood shall slay the murderer, when he meeteth him." The witnesses note that even a blow of the bare hand, if given in enmity, makes the smiter a murderer. The witnesses note that Gill notes a blow with the fist on some vital part, issuing in death, given in enmity. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him surely put to death, for he is a murderer. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the smite with his hand — even a bare-handed blow; the in enmity — struck from malice; the that he die — the fatal stroke; the the enmity the guilt — no shelter in the lack of a weapon.

The witnesses agree the law fastens finally on the enmity, not the weapon: even a bare-handed blow, struck "in enmity" and proving fatal, is murder, for the malice that prompted it is the guilt; thus no man may shelter behind the lack of a weapon if his heart and his deed were murderous, the law searching the motive to its root and holding the hateful striker guilty of blood, however slight the instrument — the enmity itself the murderer's condemnation.

So the witnesses make the verse the blow in enmity: The witnesses note the smite with his hand: even a bare-handed blow. The witnesses note the in enmity: struck from malice. The witnesses note the that he die: the fatal stroke. The witnesses note the the enmity the guilt: no shelter in the lack of a weapon. The expositors observe the smite with his hand: even a bare-handed blow. The expositors observe the in enmity: struck from malice. The expositors observe the that he die: the fatal stroke. The expositors observe the the enmity the guilt: no shelter in the lack of a weapon. The believer is taught to mark the smite with his hand, the in enmity, the that he die, the the enmity the guilt, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that even a bare-handed blow struck in enmity makes the murderer — the malice the guilt, no shelter in the lack of a weapon, the law searching the motive to its root.
22:
"But if he thrust him suddenly without enmity, or have cast upon him any thing without laying of wait," The witnesses note the contrasting case — a sudden act without enmity or ambush, by mere accident. The witnesses note that Gill notes a sudden push, before one is aware, without any malicious design, but merely through accident. The witnesses note that there was no enmity and no lying in wait. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thrust suddenly — a sudden act, unforeseen; the without enmity — no malice in it; the without laying of wait — no ambush or plot; the misadventure, not murder — the intent absent that makes murder.

The witnesses agree the law now turns to the innocent slayer, marked by the absence of malice: where the killing was sudden, without enmity, and not by ambush, it was misadventure and not murder, the deed lacking the very thing that makes murder — the hateful intent; the careful distinction shows the justice of God, who will not punish the unwilling hand as he punishes the malicious heart, but weighs the intent, sparing the one who slew by mischance the death due to the murderer.

So the witnesses make the verse the accidental killing: The witnesses note the thrust suddenly: a sudden act, unforeseen. The witnesses note the without enmity: no malice in it. The witnesses note the without laying of wait: no ambush or plot. The witnesses note the misadventure, not murder: the intent absent that makes murder. The expositors observe the thrust suddenly: a sudden act, unforeseen. The expositors observe the without enmity: no malice in it. The expositors observe the without laying of wait: no ambush or plot. The expositors observe the misadventure, not murder: the intent absent that makes murder. The believer is taught to mark the thrust suddenly, the without enmity, the without laying of wait, the misadventure, not murder, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that a sudden killing without enmity or ambush is misadventure, not murder — God weighing the intent, sparing the unwilling hand the death due to the malicious heart.
23:
"Or with any stone, wherewith a man may die, seeing him not, and cast it upon him, that he die, and was not his enemy, neither sought his harm:" The witnesses note the case of one who kills by a falling stone, not seeing the man, being no enemy nor seeking his harm. The witnesses note that Gill notes a stone sufficient to kill, but thrown or dropped without seeing the man. The witnesses note that the slayer was not his enemy, neither sought his harm. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seeing him not — the man unseen; the cast it upon him — the stone falling by accident; the not his enemy — no enmity between them; the neither sought his harm — no ill will, pure mischance.

The witnesses agree the marks of innocence are carefully named — not seeing the man, no enmity, no seeking of harm: the deadly stone here kills by pure accident, the slayer ignorant and without ill will, and so his case is wholly other than the murderer's; the law's exactness in distinguishing shows how precious is human life and how just is God, who will neither let the murderer escape nor the innocent be slain, but tries each case by the heart and the circumstances, that true justice be done.

So the witnesses make the verse killing in ignorance: The witnesses note the seeing him not: the man unseen. The witnesses note the cast it upon him: the stone falling by accident. The witnesses note the not his enemy: no enmity between them. The witnesses note the neither sought his harm: no ill will, pure mischance. The expositors observe the seeing him not: the man unseen. The expositors observe the cast it upon him: the stone falling by accident. The expositors observe the not his enemy: no enmity between them. The expositors observe the neither sought his harm: no ill will, pure mischance. The believer is taught to mark the seeing him not, the cast it upon him, the not his enemy, the neither sought his harm, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that killing by a falling stone, unseen and without enmity, is innocent mischance — the law's exactness showing how precious life is and how just God is, trying each case by the heart.
24:
"Then the congregation shall judge between the slayer and the revenger of blood according to these judgments:" The witnesses note the congregation — the court — to judge between the slayer and the avenger by these rules. The witnesses note that Gill notes the congregation is the court of judicature, assembled to hear and try the cause. The witnesses note that they were to judge according to these statutes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the congregation — the court assembled; the shall judge — weighing the cause impartially; the between slayer and avenger — deciding murder or mischance; the not by the avenger alone — the matter committed to lawful trial; the by these judgments — under God's appointed rules.

The witnesses agree the matter was committed to lawful judgment, not to the avenger's hand alone: the congregation, sitting as a court, was to weigh the case between the slayer and the avenger of blood by the rules God had given, deciding whether it was murder or mischance; the appointing of a fair trial guards both the innocent and the avenger's right, that no man die unheard nor the guilty be spared, the cause determined by impartial judgment under God's law and not by passion.

So the witnesses make the verse the congregation judges: The witnesses note the the congregation: the court assembled. The witnesses note the shall judge: weighing the cause impartially. The witnesses note the between slayer and avenger: deciding murder or mischance. The witnesses note the not by the avenger alone: the matter committed to lawful trial. The witnesses note the by these judgments: under God's appointed rules. The expositors observe the the congregation: the court assembled. The expositors observe the shall judge: weighing the cause impartially. The expositors observe the between slayer and avenger: deciding murder or mischance. The expositors observe the not by the avenger alone: the matter committed to lawful trial. The expositors observe the by these judgments: under God's appointed rules. The believer is taught to mark the the congregation, the shall judge, the between slayer and avenger, the not by the avenger alone, the by these judgments, and to reverence the God who upholds both justice and mercy. The verse thus records that the congregation was to judge between the slayer and the avenger by these rules — a fair trial under God's law, guarding both the innocent and the avenger's right, the cause determined by impartial judgment.
25:
"And the congregation shall deliver the slayer out of the hand of the revenger of blood, and the congregation shall restore him to the city of his refuge... and he shall abide in it unto the death of the high priest..." The witnesses note the slayer, judged innocent of murder, restored to the refuge to abide there till the high priest's death. The witnesses note that Gill notes the congregation put him under the care of proper persons to conduct him safely to the city, restraining the avenger. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him abiding there until the death of the high priest anointed with the holy oil. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the delivered from the avenger — the slayer kept safe; the restored to the refuge — conducted there in safety; the abide unto the death — of the high priest; the the type of Christ — freed by the High Priest's death (Heb. 9:12).

The witnesses agree the deliverance and the term of refuge carry the deepest gospel meaning of the chapter: the manslayer, cleared of murder, was kept safe in the city until the high priest died, his liberty bound up with that death; herein the fathers and the reformers see Christ, the great High Priest, by whose death the guilty are set free and restored — the sinner, fled to the refuge, released from his banishment only by the dying of the High Priest, "an eternal redemption" obtained by the death of Christ (Heb. 9:12).

So the witnesses make the verse till the high priest die: The witnesses note the delivered from the avenger: the slayer kept safe. The witnesses note the restored to the refuge: conducted there in safety. The witnesses note the abide unto the death: of the high priest. The witnesses note the the type of Christ: freed by the High Priest's death (Heb. 9:12). The expositors observe the delivered from the avenger: the slayer kept safe. The expositors observe the restored to the refuge: conducted there in safety. The expositors observe the abide unto the death: of the high priest. The expositors observe the the type of Christ: freed by the High Priest's death (Heb. 9:12). The believer is taught to mark the delivered from the avenger, the restored to the refuge, the abide unto the death, the the type of Christ, and to look to the death of the great High Priest. The verse thus records that the slayer abode in the refuge till the high priest's death — the type of Christ, the guilty set free and restored only by the dying of the great High Priest (Heb. 9:12).
26:
"But if the slayer shall at any time come without the border of the city of his refuge, whither he was fled;" The witnesses note the condition of the slayer's safety — he must not pass beyond the border of his refuge. The witnesses note that Gill notes the border seems to be the suburbs assigned to the city; within it he was safe, but the slayer who left did so at his peril. The witnesses note that the safety was bound to remaining within the refuge. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the come without the border — venturing beyond the refuge; the of his refuge — the city of his safety; the whither he was fled — where alone he was secure; the safety in abiding — as the believer abides in Christ his refuge.

The witnesses agree the safety of the refuge held only so long as the slayer stayed within it: should he venture beyond the border, he forfeited its protection; the condition teaches that the security of the fleeing sinner lies in abiding in the refuge and not straying from it, even as the believer's safety is in remaining in Christ, who is his refuge — to go out from him is to leave the only place of safety and to expose oneself again to the avenging law.

So the witnesses make the verse within the border: The witnesses note the come without the border: venturing beyond the refuge. The witnesses note the of his refuge: the city of his safety. The witnesses note the whither he was fled: where alone he was secure. The witnesses note the safety in abiding: as the believer abides in Christ his refuge. The expositors observe the come without the border: venturing beyond the refuge. The expositors observe the of his refuge: the city of his safety. The expositors observe the whither he was fled: where alone he was secure. The expositors observe the safety in abiding: as the believer abides in Christ his refuge. The believer is taught to mark the come without the border, the of his refuge, the whither he was fled, the safety in abiding, and to look to the death of the great High Priest. The verse thus records that the slayer was safe only within the border of his refuge — security in abiding and not straying, as the believer's safety is in remaining in Christ his refuge.
27:
"And the revenger of blood find him without the borders of the city of his refuge, and the revenger of blood kill the slayer; he shall not be guilty of blood:" The witnesses note that if the avenger killed the slayer found outside the refuge, he bore no guilt. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is if the avenger found him without the suburbs, fields, and vineyards belonging to the city. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the avenger guiltless in such a case. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the find him without — the slayer beyond the border; the kill the slayer — the avenger acting lawfully; the not guilty of blood — no guilt on the avenger; the his own folly — the slayer forfeiting his safety.

The witnesses agree the avenger went free of guilt because the slayer had forfeited his own safety: by leaving the refuge against the law, the manslayer exposed himself, and his blood was on his own head, the avenger but doing what the law allowed; the case underlines the seriousness of the condition — that the protection was real but conditional, and the one who despised it by straying lost it, his death the fruit of his own folly, not the avenger's wrong.

So the witnesses make the verse the avenger guiltless: The witnesses note the find him without: the slayer beyond the border. The witnesses note the kill the slayer: the avenger acting lawfully. The witnesses note the not guilty of blood: no guilt on the avenger. The witnesses note the his own folly: the slayer forfeiting his safety. The expositors observe the find him without: the slayer beyond the border. The expositors observe the kill the slayer: the avenger acting lawfully. The expositors observe the not guilty of blood: no guilt on the avenger. The expositors observe the his own folly: the slayer forfeiting his safety. The believer is taught to mark the find him without, the kill the slayer, the not guilty of blood, the his own folly, and to look to the death of the great High Priest. The verse thus records that the avenger who killed the straying slayer bore no guilt — the manslayer forfeiting his own safety, his death the fruit of his folly in despising the refuge.
28:
"Because he should have remained in the city of his refuge until the death of the high priest: but after the death of the high priest the slayer shall return into the land of his possession." The witnesses note that the slayer was bound to the refuge till the high priest's death, after which he might freely return home. The witnesses note that Gill notes nothing but the high priest's death could give him liberty; the merciful provision yet carried some appearance of punishment, the man confined within the city's bounds. The witnesses note that after that death he returned to his possession. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the remained in the refuge — bound there till the high priest died; the a kind of punishment — confined within the city; the after the death — set free to return; the restored to his possession — the inheritance regained by that death (Heb. 9:15).

The witnesses agree the term and the release together preach the gospel plainly: the slayer's confinement was real, a kind of punishment, and only the high priest's death could free him to return to his inheritance; so the sinner, sheltered in his refuge, waits for the death of the great High Priest, by which alone he is set free and restored to his forfeited possession — the death of Christ opening the prison and giving the guilty their liberty and their inheritance again (cf. Heb. 9:15).

So the witnesses make the verse freed by that death: The witnesses note the remained in the refuge: bound there till the high priest died. The witnesses note the a kind of punishment: confined within the city. The witnesses note the after the death: set free to return. The witnesses note the restored to his possession: the inheritance regained by that death (Heb. 9:15). The expositors observe the remained in the refuge: bound there till the high priest died. The expositors observe the a kind of punishment: confined within the city. The expositors observe the after the death: set free to return. The expositors observe the restored to his possession: the inheritance regained by that death (Heb. 9:15). The believer is taught to mark the remained in the refuge, the a kind of punishment, the after the death, the restored to his possession, and to look to the death of the great High Priest. The verse thus records that the slayer, freed by the high priest's death, returned to his possession — the sinner restored by the death of the great High Priest, his liberty and inheritance regained by the death of Christ (Heb. 9:15).
29:
"So these things shall be for a statute of judgment unto you throughout your generations in all your dwellings." The witnesses note these laws made a perpetual statute of judgment for all generations. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was a judicial law, according to which they were to proceed in all the above cases. The witnesses note that it bound all their generations in all their dwellings. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a statute of judgment — a standing judicial rule; the throughout your generations — binding every age; the in all your dwellings — wherever they lived; the justice never to lapse — god's steady care for righteous judgment.

The witnesses agree the law was no temporary measure but a standing rule for the nation's life: the handling of bloodshed — the cities of refuge, the trial, the distinction of murder and mischance — was fixed as a perpetual statute, binding every generation wherever they dwelt; the permanence of it shows how gravely God regarded the shedding of blood and how steadily he would have justice and mercy administered, the rule never to lapse but to govern Israel's common life throughout their days.

So the witnesses make the verse a perpetual statute: The witnesses note the a statute of judgment: a standing judicial rule. The witnesses note the throughout your generations: binding every age. The witnesses note the in all your dwellings: wherever they lived. The witnesses note the justice never to lapse: God's steady care for righteous judgment. The expositors observe the a statute of judgment: a standing judicial rule. The expositors observe the throughout your generations: binding every age. The expositors observe the in all your dwellings: wherever they lived. The expositors observe the justice never to lapse: God's steady care for righteous judgment. The believer is taught to mark the a statute of judgment, the throughout your generations, the in all your dwellings, the justice never to lapse, and to look to the death of the great High Priest. The verse thus records that these laws were a perpetual statute of judgment for all generations — God's steady care that justice and mercy in bloodshed govern Israel's life throughout their days.
30:
"Whoso killeth any person, the murderer shall be put to death by the mouth of witnesses: but one witness shall not testify against any person to cause him to die." The witnesses note the murderer put to death only on the testimony of witnesses, never on one alone. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of one who kills willingly, through enmity and malice. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the rule — by the mouth of witnesses, but one witness shall not suffice for death. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the murderer put to death — justice firm against murder; the by the mouth of witnesses — on sure testimony; the not one witness — never on a single word; the safe against wrong — none to die but on certain proof (Deut. 19:15).

The witnesses agree the requiring of more than one witness guarded life against false or mistaken accusation: no man, even a murderer, was to be put to death on a single testimony, but only on the word of two or more, that the truth be sure before blood was shed; the rule shows God's care that justice be both firm against murder and safe against wrong, the sanctity of life protecting even the accused, that none die but on certain proof (cf. Deut. 19:15).

So the witnesses make the verse by the mouth of witnesses: The witnesses note the the murderer put to death: justice firm against murder. The witnesses note the by the mouth of witnesses: on sure testimony. The witnesses note the not one witness: never on a single word. The witnesses note the safe against wrong: none to die but on certain proof (Deut. 19:15). The expositors observe the the murderer put to death: justice firm against murder. The expositors observe the by the mouth of witnesses: on sure testimony. The expositors observe the not one witness: never on a single word. The expositors observe the safe against wrong: none to die but on certain proof (Deut. 19:15). The believer is taught to mark the the murderer put to death, the by the mouth of witnesses, the not one witness, the safe against wrong, and to look to the death of the great High Priest. The verse thus records that the murderer was put to death only on the mouth of witnesses, never one alone — justice firm against murder yet safe against wrong, none to die but on certain proof (Deut. 19:15).
31:
"Moreover ye shall take no satisfaction for the life of a murderer, which is guilty of death: but he shall be surely put to death." The witnesses note that no ransom might be taken for a murderer's life — he must surely die. The witnesses note that Gill notes though he would give all his wealth and substance, no satisfaction was to be taken; the life of such a man was not to be saved by money. The witnesses note that he was guilty of death and must surely be put to death. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no satisfaction — no ransom to be taken; the guilty of death — his life forfeit; the surely put to death — justice not to be bribed; the the worth of life — blood answered by blood (Gen. 9:6).

The witnesses agree the forbidding of any ransom marks the absolute sanctity of human life: no wealth, however great, could buy off the murderer, for his life was forfeit and justice would not be bribed; the law sets the value of a man's life beyond all price, so that the deliberate taking of it must be answered with the murderer's own life and nothing less — a witness to the worth God sets on the life made in his image, "whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed" (Gen. 9:6).

So the witnesses make the verse no ransom for the murderer: The witnesses note the no satisfaction: no ransom to be taken. The witnesses note the guilty of death: his life forfeit. The witnesses note the surely put to death: justice not to be bribed. The witnesses note the the worth of life: blood answered by blood (Gen. 9:6). The expositors observe the no satisfaction: no ransom to be taken. The expositors observe the guilty of death: his life forfeit. The expositors observe the surely put to death: justice not to be bribed. The expositors observe the the worth of life: blood answered by blood (Gen. 9:6). The believer is taught to mark the no satisfaction, the guilty of death, the surely put to death, the the worth of life, and to look to the death of the great High Priest. The verse thus records that no ransom might be taken for a murderer's life — the absolute sanctity of human life, justice not to be bribed, blood answered by blood (Gen. 9:6).
32:
"And ye shall take no satisfaction for him that is fled to the city of his refuge, that he should come again to dwell in the land, until the death of the priest." The witnesses note that not even the manslayer might buy an early return — he must wait the priest's death. The witnesses note that Gill notes no satisfaction was to be taken even for him that fled for killing a man unawares. The witnesses note that Clarke sees this intended to typify that no sinner can be delivered from his banishment from God, or recover his forfeited inheritance, till Jesus Christ the great High Priest had died for his offences and risen again. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no satisfaction — not even for the manslayer; the come again to dwell — no buying an early return; the until the death of the priest — the appointed term alone; the the type of Christ — only the High Priest's death frees the sinner.

The Wesleyan reader (Clarke) draws out the gospel openly: as the manslayer could not buy his way home but must wait the high priest's death, so no sinner can be delivered from his banishment from God or recover his forfeited inheritance until the great High Priest, Jesus Christ, had died for his offences and risen again; the law forbidding any ransom for an early release fixes the eye on that one death alone as the ground of the guilty's liberty — no price of ours, but the death of Christ, restores us to God.

So the witnesses make the verse no early ransom: The witnesses note the no satisfaction: not even for the manslayer. The witnesses note the come again to dwell: no buying an early return. The witnesses note the until the death of the priest: the appointed term alone. The witnesses note the the type of Christ: only the High Priest's death frees the sinner. The expositors observe the no satisfaction: not even for the manslayer. The expositors observe the come again to dwell: no buying an early return. The expositors observe the until the death of the priest: the appointed term alone. The expositors observe the the type of Christ: only the High Priest's death frees the sinner. The believer is taught to mark the no satisfaction, the come again to dwell, the until the death of the priest, the the type of Christ, and to look to the death of the great High Priest. The verse thus records that not even the manslayer could buy an early return, but must wait the priest's death — typifying that only the death of Christ the great High Priest delivers the sinner and restores his inheritance.
33:
"So ye shall not pollute the land wherein ye are: for blood it defileth the land: and the land cannot be cleansed of the blood that is shed therein, but by the blood of him that shed it." The witnesses note that blood pollutes the land, and only the blood of the murderer can cleanse it. The witnesses note that Gill notes the land was not to be polluted, as it had been by the old inhabitants through idolatry, adultery, and murder. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the land was considered guilty till the murderer's blood was shed in it, no wonder God is so strict against murderers. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not pollute the land — bloodshed defiling it; the blood defileth — the guilt cleaving to the land; the cleansed... by the blood — only the murderer's blood purges it; the answered by blood — the deeper defilement cleansed in Christ (Heb. 9:22).

The witnesses agree this verse fixes the gravest truth of the chapter — that bloodshed defiles the very land, and the defilement is purged only by the blood of him who shed it: unpunished murder leaves a guilt on the land that no other satisfaction can remove; the principle both demands the execution of the murderer and points beyond it, for the shedding of innocent blood can be answered only by blood — fulfilled at last in the blood of Christ, which alone cleanses the deeper defilement of sin that no human punishment could purge (cf. Heb. 9:22, without shedding of blood is no remission).

So the witnesses make the verse blood defiles the land: The witnesses note the not pollute the land: bloodshed defiling it. The witnesses note the blood defileth: the guilt cleaving to the land. The witnesses note the cleansed... by the blood: only the murderer's blood purges it. The witnesses note the answered by blood: the deeper defilement cleansed in Christ (Heb. 9:22). The expositors observe the not pollute the land: bloodshed defiling it. The expositors observe the blood defileth: the guilt cleaving to the land. The expositors observe the cleansed... by the blood: only the murderer's blood purges it. The expositors observe the answered by blood: the deeper defilement cleansed in Christ (Heb. 9:22). The believer is taught to mark the not pollute the land, the blood defileth, the cleansed... by the blood, the answered by blood, and to look to the death of the great High Priest. The verse thus records that blood defiles the land, cleansed only by the blood of him that shed it — the gravity of bloodshed, answered only by blood, the deeper defilement of sin cleansed at last in Christ (Heb. 9:22).
34:
"Defile not therefore the land which ye shall inherit, wherein I dwell: for I the LORD dwell among the children of Israel." The witnesses note the final reason — the land must not be defiled because the LORD himself dwells in it among his people. The witnesses note that Gill notes the exhortation is strengthened by this — the Holy God dwells there, as in the tabernacle erected among them. The witnesses note that the defiling of the land was the more grievous because of his presence. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the defile not the land — keep it clean of blood; the which ye shall inherit — the land of their possession; the wherein I dwell — the LORD in the midst; the the ground of holiness — his presence requiring a clean people (2 Cor. 6:16).

The witnesses agree the crowning motive for purity is the presence of God: the land was not to be defiled with unpunished blood because the LORD himself dwelt in the midst of it, and his holiness could not abide with such pollution; the reason reaches beyond the law of murder to the whole life of the people — that those among whom the holy God dwells must keep themselves and their land clean, the indwelling presence the great ground and motive of all holiness (cf. 2 Cor. 6:16, I will dwell in them... be ye clean).

So the witnesses make the verse God dwells among them: The witnesses note the defile not the land: keep it clean of blood. The witnesses note the which ye shall inherit: the land of their possession. The witnesses note the wherein I dwell: the LORD in the midst. The witnesses note the the ground of holiness: his presence requiring a clean people (2 Cor. 6:16). The expositors observe the defile not the land: keep it clean of blood. The expositors observe the which ye shall inherit: the land of their possession. The expositors observe the wherein I dwell: the LORD in the midst. The expositors observe the the ground of holiness: his presence requiring a clean people (2 Cor. 6:16). The believer is taught to mark the defile not the land, the which ye shall inherit, the wherein I dwell, the the ground of holiness, and to look to the death of the great High Priest. The verse thus records that the land must not be defiled because the LORD dwells in it — his presence the crowning motive of holiness, those among whom the holy God dwells to keep themselves and their land clean (2 Cor. 6:16).
